item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're totally right about Keter class, but Apollyon is a bit different. It basically means "Its gonna destroy the world and we can't stop it." Its mainly used for things like 001 proposals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So we have an unstoppable pony making a commotion in the midst of a crowded station?

Is this the most harmless keter on the website?? …It should be keter, right? Or the special containment procedures need to be more detailed. It seems so far, despite everything we tried, SCP-2965 WILL do what it does, with its mechanical parts with little humans (fairies from the long corgi scp?), and wall phasing, and at least every 3 iterations, in the middle of goddamn rush hour. I would joke about making this apollyon, but I'm genuinely a newb asking stupid questions about object classes and waste people's time.

As I write this, I realized the time was changed from 20 hours to 8 days. It does make it a bit more reasonable to close down this station once a week, rather than close for an hour every day. Hmm…
I guess I'm just trying to say it's super interesting to see something so harmless yet hard to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I'm one of them :)

But while I'm here let me make use of my time.

YES SINGAPORE IS RECOGNISED
(Does anyone really think about the extreme difficulties of a foundation base here because literally everywhere has people there or is it just me)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my mind this is related to 2952 somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be Keter? I mean, the foundation can't contain it except for making sure it gets to its destination, and where it spawns is random. Other than that, solid article, funny and well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are literally dozens of us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first SCP I've seen set in Singapore. Kudos to you, friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Checked again and it's fine now. Disregard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to be working fine from my machine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heads up to the author and other concerned parties: the image for this page appears to be broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
19 hours ago, user FlutterBrave edited the following in:

The wall used as the point of entry does not appear to suffer any physical damage as a result of SCP-2965's manifestation, although as if SCP-2965 is was entirely corporeal.

Edit has been reverted. SCP-2965 is entirely corporeal, although its manifestation appears to suggest otherwise. For the record, it's strongly suggested to consult with the author before making such edits to any piece of writing on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, in part because of nostalgia points (a lot of Series I is like this), and in part because I just like the idea. There are quite a few good "long" SCPs, but a lot of the ones that stick with you have a simple premise, even if that makes them slightly "goofy". Sometimes anomalies aren't _K-class-causing things-man-was-not-meant-to-know. Sometimes they are just subway ponies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that it's starting to get annoyed with all these idiots being in the way.

item.title: Feels a bit off
item.content:encoded: Two things I feel are missing from the article are the results of attempts to communicate with the horse/train and the people inside, and recommendations regarding either more civilized attempts to interact with it to reduce it's hostility or at the very least safety precautions for people intentionally exposed to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I have an irrational fear of Singapore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That makes the three of us then. Huh.

Anyway, I wrote this literally overnight without bothering to consult any sources or my own memory, so that explains the platform naming error. As for "metro system", I'm using what seems to be an international term, as opposed to a local term that warrants an explanation… no harm changing to MRT and providing a footnote, but I also don't see any problem in not changing.

And it's completely obvious it's Dhoby Ghaut, once you see Circle Line, Platform 6 and two five letter words as the blackboxed name. I wanted it to be a bonus "haha, I know this" moment, for literally anyone who takes the MRT here.

Why isn't this better contained? Who cares? It's a tiny horse with tiny people inside, rampaging on an ultramodern train station packed with commuters on National Day, of all days, while a disembodied voice obliviously warns commuters to Stand Behind The Yellow Line, Please Mind The Platform Gap, beepbeepbeepbeepbeepbeep. I could use the disused platform at Harbourfront though, mind posting its designation here? Like platform A or B or whatever.

(fun fact: 2965 is not a random number. I wrote this over the SG50 break. Best time to post the first scip located in the Finest City in the World, baby.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with the above for the most part.

Furthermore,

Singapore's underground metro system

Singapore don't really call it "metro system". A proper in-context fit is "Mass Rapid Transit" (MRT).

Point-of-Exit at Platform 6 of the Circle Line without incident

As said above, platforms are named by letters. And if there's 6 of them and based in Circle Line, it is most likely Dhoby Ghaut (which intersects three lines currently).

Aside: Yeah! Fellow Singaporean!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just made me go "wat", especially given that:

- I'm a brony
- I'm from Singapore
- I'm a railfan

It's like this SCP speaks to me. lol.

Nitpick: in Singapore, except for internal purposes train platforms are referred to by letters. Also, each line's platforms has their own set of letters, except for platforms used by multiple lines in a cross-platform interchange transfer setup (City Hall, Raffles Place, Bayfront, and Jurong East stations as of now). So for example, at Dhoby Ghaut, the North-South Line platforms are lettered A and B, separately from the North-East Line platforms which are also A and B, and again separately from the Circle Line platforms which are also A and B. Confusing, I know.
You might want to change it to "Platform A" or "Platform B of the Circle Line".

Here's an idea: one of the terminus station on the Circle Line, HarbourFront, is also an interchange station with another line; on the Circle Line side there is a platform, designed to be used as an alighting-only terminating platform but, as far as I know, is never used in practice. The platform's partly cordoned off and staff will guide you to the "right" platform if you stray there. You might want to "relocate" the anomaly there if you want to; I can try supplying photos of that particular platform and its letter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A perfectly dispassionate accounting of something utterly ridiculous. +1
It's short, sweet, clever, to the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything is connected to the Buried Giant.

The Buried Giant may or may not be God in my headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…sorry, no, that's definitely not it. I only really see 2211 as being marginally part of ophite's "we go into the real world of ovens and dishwashers when we die" schtick, but I'm tickled that you perceive a link between this and Buried Giant.

These people are simply taking the tube.

(And while I'm at it, image credits go to user supernutorcrazy on this site .)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does one of these passengers have a cousin with a propensity for getting his arm stuck in ovens?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very delightful. The fact that sells it to me is that for these people inside the horse, this is perfectly normal, and there's nothing inherently weird or strange about being naked or inside a horse, and that it's passing through another train station to get these people to their destinations. The fact that it can't be slowed down at all, while probably a necessary part of the story, does bother me somewhat, but I can buy it with the other strengths this displays.

Agreeing with Scantron; you really should change that 12,006 minutes bit, as I can't get a good idea of what that is in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is goofy as hell all-around, in a charming way, and I like the feel that this is just some standard part of the universe that simply fails to comply with our normal. On top of that, it's competently written without unnecessary frills, so +1.

I would suggest changing 12006 minutes to 20 hours and 6 minutes, though.
